title: Phishing Attack on a Company
description: You are a red team operator executing a phishing campaign against a target corporation. Navigate through the attack lifecycle using real-world tactics.

phases.name: Phase 1: Reconnaissance
phases.narrative: You've been contracted to test a company's defenses. Your first step is reconnaissance. You need to gather information about the target organization before launching the attack.

choices.text: Use LinkedIn to identify employees and their roles
choices.outcome: Excellent! You find the CFO, IT Manager, and several finance employees. This gives you perfect targets for spear-phishing. You also discover the company uses Office 365.

choices.text: Immediately run Nmap scan against company's IP range
choices.outcome: Too aggressive! The company's IDS detects your scan and logs your IP. You've burned your recon opportunity and alerted defenders. Points deducted.

choices.text: Perform WHOIS lookups and DNS enumeration
choices.outcome: Good passive reconnaissance. You discover mail servers, subdomains, and the company's email format (firstname.lastname@company.com). Solid foundation.

phases.name: Phase 2: Weaponization
phases.narrative: Now you need to craft your phishing payload. You know the company uses Office 365 and the employees' names. How will you craft your attack?

choices.text: Create a spear-phishing email targeting the CFO with a fake invoice
choices.outcome: Smart targeting! The CFO receives a convincing email appearing to be from a known vendor. You craft a macro-enabled Excel file with a fake invoice. High-value target selected.

choices.text: Send bulk phishing emails to all employees
choices.outcome: Mass campaign detected by email filters. 95% blocked. One employee reports it to IT immediately. You've raised the alarm with minimal gain.

choices.text: Create a convincing Office 365 credential harvesting page
choices.outcome: Excellent technique! You clone the O365 login page hosted on a convincing domain. This is a proven credential harvesting method.

phases.name: Phase 3: Delivery
phases.narrative: Your payload is ready. Now you need to deliver it effectively. The target's email uses Microsoft Defender for Office 365.

choices.text: Spoof a trusted vendor email address with your payload
choices.outcome: The spoofed email bypasses basic filters. The CFO opens the attachment thinking it's from a known vendor. Payload delivered successfully!

choices.text: Send a link to your credential harvesting page disguised as an O365 login
choices.outcome: The link is flagged by Defender's Safe Links feature. The URL is detonated in a sandbox and marked as phishing. Delivery failed.

choices.text: Drop infected USB drives in the company parking lot
choices.outcome: Creative physical delivery! Two employees plug in the USBs. The payload executes on one machine. Slow but effective.

phases.name: Phase 4: Exploitation
phases.narrative: The payload has been delivered. The CFO opened the Excel file. A macro prompt appeared. What do you hope happens next, and what's your backup plan?

choices.text: Wait for the CFO to enable macros and execute the payload
choices.outcome: The CFO enables macros (it IS an invoice they were expecting). Your payload executes, establishing a reverse shell. Initial access achieved!

choices.text: Send a follow-up email pressuring the CFO to 'enable content' immediately
choices.outcome: The follow-up raises suspicion. The CFO forwards both emails to IT Security. Your campaign is now being investigated. You triggered an incident.

choices.text: Use harvested credentials to log into O365 directly
choices.outcome: You successfully log into the CFO's O365 account. Access to emails, SharePoint, and Teams. Highly valuable initial foothold!

phases.name: Mission Complete
phases.narrative: You have completed the Red Team phishing engagement. Your report will document the vulnerabilities exploited and provide recommendations to the defensive team.
